**image-processing protocol**

A. **Illumination Correction.**

```{=html}
<!-- -->
```
1. This needs to be done on the local system, so we copy all the .rcpnl
 > files from all the cycles and store them under a Folder called
 > 'raw'.

- This has been done in Linux workstation in the old office. It is
 > suggested to copy the rcpnl files to the workstation and not to
 > use hard drive when running the codes. Rcpnl files are produced in
 > scanning and each cycle of the slide has its own rcpnl

--- Project_folder

--- raw

--- sample 1

--- cycle1 rcpnl

--- cycle2 rcpnl

--- cycle3 rcpnl

..........so on...

--- sample 2

--- cycle1 rcpnl

--- cycle2 rcpnl

--- cycle3 rcpnl

..........so on...

→ no need for subfolders! so in the folder #raw are just all the rcpnls
from each cycle.

2. Go to GitHub and download all the 4 code files under our project
 > folder -
 > [[https://github.com/farkkilab/image_processing/tree/main/pipeline/0_illumination_correction]{.underline}](https://github.com/farkkilab/image_processing/tree/main/pipeline/0_illumination_correction)

3. Create 'illumination' folder

4\. The file structure should look like this under our project folder-

File structure under project folder

./

├── BaSiC_run.sh

├── Dockerfile

├── illumination **#output folder we created**

├── imagej_basic_ashlar_filepattern.py

├── imagej_basic_ashlar.py

ure under project folder

./

├── BaSiC_run.sh

├── Dockerfile

├── illumination **#output folder we created**

├── imagej_basic_ashlar_filepattern.py

├── imagej_basic_ashlar.py

└── raw **#input folder we created**

-No need to make sub folders for each sample under 'raw', we can have as
many rcpnl files under it and it will do its thing.

5\. Right click on this folder and open terminal

To check the files paste this in terminal: ls -l

Then this:

6\. Make sure that the docker app is open and running - if not search it
on the search bar and open the app

Paste this on the terminal: docker run \--privileged -it -m 120g
\--cpus=20 \--mount type=bind,source=\"\$(pwd)\",target=/data
my-basic-image bash

\# start your docker image

7\. If it says Fiji something then we are inside the docker.

So we type - bash /data/BaSiC_run.sh

(- If we get an error for the 'illumination' folder its mostly a
permission thing because we make it on the local disk so we type 'chmod
777 /illumination/' on the terminal to get rid of the restriction. )

8\. After this runs, we get 2 .tiff files (dfp and ffp) under
'illumination' for each sample.

9\. We can now make individual sample files and move all the rcpnl
respectively to their folders.

--- illumination

--- sample1

--- dfp

--- ffp

--- sample2

--- dfp

--- ffp

**B. Ashlar - stitching**

1.

al sample files and move all the rcpnl
respectively to their folders.

--- dfp

**B. Ashlar - stitching**

1. Now that we have 'raw' folder that has all the rcpnl from all the
 > cycles, We can run ashlar for all samples together. This process
 > will take some time.

2\. Now download the ashlar py code under the project folder -
[[https://github.com/farkkilab/image_processing/tree/main/pipeline/1_stitching]{.underline}](https://github.com/farkkilab/image_processing/tree/main/pipeline/1_stitching)

3\. Create a folder named 'images' under the project folder

4\. Remember the 'illumination' folder from the previous step? Now move
that folder under this project folder.

Structure should look like -

Under CIN2 project folder

--- Images

--- illumination

--- sample1

--- dfp

--- ffp

--- sample2

--- dfp

--- ffp

--- ashlar_workflow.py

--- raw

--- sample 1

--- cycles rcpnl

--- sample 2

--- cycles rcpnl

5\. Open the py script and change the paths to our folders.

- If you have done illumination correction then mark 'Y' in the
 > script, if not then change it to 'N'

6\. Right click on folder and open terminal

7\. conda activate ashlar

8\. python ashlar_workflow.py -c 1 (This is originally 42 and its the
thread. We can change it to 1 for better run)

click on folder and open terminal

7\. conda activate ashlar

8\. python ashlar_workflow.py -c 1 (This is originally 42 and its the
thread. We can change it to 1 for better run)

- step 7 and 8 are also mentioned on the py script.

9\. After this the ome.tif image is created under 'images'

**C. Segmentation**

1. Download both the py file under the project folder -
 > [[https://github.com/farkkilab/image_processing/tree/main/pipeline/2_segmentation]{.underline}](https://github.com/farkkilab/image_processing/tree/main/pipeline/2_segmentation)

2. Create a folder named 'masks'

3. The project folder should look something like:

Under CIN2 project folder

--- images

--- illumination

--- ashlar_workflow.py

--- raw

--- masks

--- stardist_segmentation.ipynb

--- stardist_segmentation.py

4\. Update the paths in the py script and right click to open terminal

5\. conda activate stardist

6\. python stardist_segmentation.py

- step 5 and 6 are also mentioned on the py script.

- Do you want to proceed? Click 'Yes' for the first time and if it
 > asks again, then click 'No'

**D. Quantification**

1. Download the py file under the project folder like above -
 > [[https://github.com/farkkilab/image_processing/tree/main/pipeline/3_quantification]{.underline}](https://github.com/farkkilab/image_processing/tree/main/pipeline/3_quantification)

2.

tps://github.com/farkkilab/image_processing/tree/main/pipeline/3_quantification]{.underline}](https://github.com/farkkilab/image_processing/tree/main/pipeline/3_quantification)

2. Create folder 'csv' under the project folder.

3. Open Libre office (the excel kind) and copy all the channels (all
 > the antibody list and paste it on the first column of the new
 > document and save it as 'channels' and it should be in the csv
 > format.

- If there are blank cells cause some cycles don't have all channels,
 > remove the blank cells.

- Rename all the DNA as DNA1, DNA2, DNA3 and so on in the
 > 'channels.csv'

- Make sure that the images have been scanned for all the cycles and
 > channels or we will get an error cause the logic is looking for
 > all the channels we have inputted in the 'channels.csv'. In that
 > case, exclude the samples from both masks and images that don't
 > have all the cycles and channels and then run quantification.

Under CIN2 project folder

--- csv

--- Images

--- Illumination

--- ashlar_workflow.py

--- raw

--- Masks

--- channels.csv

--- quantification_workflow.py

---stardist_segmentation.ipynb

--- stardist_segmentation.py

1. Edit the path in the script

2. conda activate quantification

3. python quantification_workflow.py \--outputFolder
 > /media/oncosys/CIN2_2/01_Pre-processing/seg_issue_sample04/csv
 > \--channelNamesFile

pt

2. conda activate quantification

3. python quantification_workflow.py \--outputFolder
 > /media/oncosys/CIN2_2/01_Pre-processing/seg_issue_sample04/csv
 > \--channelNamesFile
 > /media/oncosys/CIN2_2/01_Pre-processing/seg_issue_sample04/channels.csv
 > \--threads 1

- make changes to the directory

- The output is stored in the 'csv' folder.

**E. Napari or Avivator**

- Open Avivator on web and open the one.tiff under the 'images' to
 > check the quality of the images

**F. Cylinter**

1. Create a folder named 'cylinter' in the same project folder.

2. Create folders 'mask' ,'seg' and 'tif' under the cylinder folder.

3. Also copy the 'csv' folder here.

4. Copy mask ome.tif image and paste it under 'mask' and 'seg' and then
 > 'images' ome.tif to 'tiff'

5. Create a file named 'markers.csv' under cylinder folder with the
 > format below:

First column name 'channel_number': in the rows start typing 1 for the
first marker and continue numbering all your markers in serial number.

Second column name 'cycle_number': Markers used in each cycle will have
corresponding cycle number , this can be copied from experimental design
excel in google drive.

Third column name 'marker_name':List all the markers in order that were
used for the experiment.

\<INPUT DIR\>

├── cylinter_config.yml

├── csv/

│ ├── \<sample1\>.csv

│ └── \<sample2\>.csv

├── markers.csv

├── mask/

he markers in order that were
used for the experiment.

\<INPUT DIR\>

├── cylinter_config.yml

├── csv/

│ ├── \<sample1\>.csv

│ └── \<sample2\>.csv

├── markers.csv

├── mask/

│ ├── \<sample1\>.ome.tif (or .tif)

│ └── \<sample2\>.ome.tif (or .tif)

├── seg/

│ ├── \<sample1\>.ome.tif (or .tif)

│ └── \<sample2\>.ome.tif (or .tif)

└── tif/

├── \<sample1\>.ome.tif (or .tif)

└── \<sample2\>.ome.tif (or .tif []{.mark}

5\. Make sure Cylinter is downloaded.

6\. Go to
[[https://github.com/labsyspharm/cylinter/tree/master/cylinter]{.underline}](https://github.com/labsyspharm/cylinter/tree/master/cylinter)
and download cylinter_config.yml in the cylinder folder.

7\. Open it with notepad and change the path of inDir and outDir to
where your cylinter folder is located.

8\. Change the sampleMetadata: to your samples name for each of your
sample eg:

· \"1\": \[\"1\", \"Normal kidney cortex\", \"NKC\", \"CANCER-FALSE\",
1\] To

\"S263_pOme\": \[\"S263_pOme\", \"Normal kidney cortex\", \"NKC\",
\"CANCER-FALSE\", 1\]

\- every sample needs to have its own line.

9\. Change samplesForROISelection:

· \[\"1\", \"16\", \"30\", \"46\",\] to \[\"S263_pOme\", \"S345_pAdnR\",
\"S371_pAdnL\", \"S388_pOme\", \"S404_iAdn\", \"S458_pAdnL\",
"S460_iOme"\]

**10.** Open anaconda prompt and open as administrator (THIS IS VERY
IMPORTANT)

me\", \"S345_pAdnR\",
\"S371_pAdnL\", \"S388_pOme\", \"S404_iAdn\", \"S458_pAdnL\",
"S460_iOme"\]

**10.** Open anaconda prompt and open as administrator (THIS IS VERY
IMPORTANT)

-Ella: Just write conda in the terminal of cylinder folder

11\. Conda activate cylinter_SPACE

- Ella: using the Linux workstation just conda activate cylinter

12\. Make sure you are inside the directory of the cylinder folder.

11\. Type - cylinter cylinter_config.yml

\- Make sure that the markers in marker.csv is same as in the csv folder
like spelling or numbers or whatever.

**How to work on Cylinter:**

\- After running the command prompt, you should see something like INFO:
Reading configuration file and importing sample and all that and a
window pops up (it might take 2 mins)

- Adjust the DNA contrast to cyan so you can see the cells and then
 > check image for tissue folds or aggregates and anything that does
 > not look good.

- IMPORTANT: First check the segmentation of cells.

- Adjust the contrast and gamma slides to see if things are looking
 > good.

- Once you find spots that don't look good, click on Manual ROI
 > selection on the left side, right above the channel list.

- You can check the segmentation mask to see if there is anything
 > missing, if the segmentation is lacking in certain regions, you
 > should probably take it off.

hannel list.

- You can check the segmentation mask to see if there is anything
 > missing, if the segmentation is lacking in certain regions, you
 > should probably take it off.

- Select the upside down M kinda thing (top right) to map the areas
 > and double click to finish the selection. Repeat this process on
 > all regions we would like to exclude.

- Check every channel to remove any aggregates and anything noisy and
 > use green so you can visualize better against the cyan DNA
 > channel.

 - Ella: DNA red and channel yellow

- Once we have gone through all the channels and have done the ROI
 > selection, click on 'apply ROI' on the right and then wait until
 > the next image pops up to repeat the whole process all over again.

- If the system starts to hang, you can always close the window and
 > open it but you will have to pass through all the steps by
 > clicking on 'apply roi' on the right.

- If we want to jump to a specific module, we can use module specific
 > command -\>

cylinter - - module selectROIs cylinter_config.yml (This selectROIs can
change depending on what stage of the process we are in) (If any doubt
or command is not working, check out cylinter website)

- IMPORTANT : After selecting ROI for the last image make sure you
 > allow the terminal to run and do its things cause it needs to
 > create a folder called 'selectROIs'.

site)

- IMPORTANT : After selecting ROI for the last image make sure you
 > allow the terminal to run and do its things cause it needs to
 > create a folder called 'selectROIs'.

- After selectROIs is saved, Cylinder will pop up again and we need to
 > do intensityFilter, areaFilter and cycleCorrelation modules.

```{=html}
<!-- -->
```
- These 3 steps are easy, you just need to adjust the range to the
 > base of the grey mountain.

- Once that is done, click on 'plot points'

- And then click on 'apply gates' and move to the next sample.

- Close the Cylinter window after all the required modules are done
 > and now all these files are stored under the 'checkpoints'

- IMPORTANT

> To extract CSV files by aggregating Cylinter checkpoints: run\
> ./pipeline/Read_parquet_checkpoint.ipynb

**Things learnt from Angela**

- Check cycle by cycle to cut things out and if they keep showing up
 > in every cycle then its not supposed to be there so cut it out for
 > example bright spots but if these spots intensify gradually, don't
 > cut them out cause they just might be a cluster of immune cells
 > that soaked up the staining nicely.

- If the border of the sample is bright compared to other parts just
 > remove that as well.

- Do not have to remove blurry background.

- Don't need to have DNA channel switched on when checking other
 > channels.

ht compared to other parts just
 > remove that as well.

- Do not have to remove blurry background.

- Don't need to have DNA channel switched on when checking other
 > channels.

- If you are unsure if you should cut it out or not, switch on
 > 'segmentation' to see if it identified the cells and then we can
 > do.

**G. Gating or TRIBUS**

- It is recommended to try Tribus on the samples first.

- After that, we can check the masks created with the marker
 > expression to check if phenotyping is done correctly.

- Check for **every sample** and if you feel like it has not been
 > phenotyped for some samples you can gate them only.

- But if you start seeing patterns for a specific type of marker, then
 > its best to gate all the samples for that particular maker(s).

**FOR CIN2:** With Ada we checked the images and we saw that there was
lot of marker spillover which hindered our gating with tribus for some
of the markers, so we decided to manually gate those.

**TRIBUS:**

1\. Installing Tribus and setting it up for your system.

- This can be done on your personal laptop.

- Open terminal and then type - git clone
 > https://github.com/farkkilab/tribus.git

- Move into tribus directory by typing - cd tribus

- Will look like this:

LM4-945-31364:\~ gurupras\$ git clone
https://github.com/farkkilab/tribus.git

Cloning into \'tribus\'\...

ve into tribus directory by typing - cd tribus

- Will look like this:

LM4-945-31364:\~ gurupras\$ git clone
https://github.com/farkkilab/tribus.git

Cloning into \'tribus\'\...

- Install Anaconda

- Creating a virtual env and installing everything inside the the
 > conda directly.

conda create -n tribus_env python=3.10 -y

conda activate tribus_env

- Install everything needed

conda install numpy pandas scipy scikit-learn matplotlib seaborn jupyter
-y

conda install -c conda-forge umap-learn leidenalg -y

- Finally installing Tribus

python setup.py develop

- To test if everything has worked

python tribus/tribus.py \--help

- If this throws errors, you can install the required packages by
 > using 'pip install module_name'

**Press Enter**

[2. Creating the Logic table -]{.mark}

- [This really depends on what type of cells you would like to see and
 > the markers important in our project.]{.mark}

- [So refer the excel sheet of the project to see what we would like
 > to see and make tables.]{.mark}

- [And then giving it values based on its presence and assigning -1,0
 > or 1]{.mark}

[Read more about this here - https://github.com/farkkilab/tribus]{.mark}

- [Once we have Tribus and the logic table ready we can now launch the
 > Jupyter notebook - type 'jupyter notebook' and a pop up opens and
 > we select the folder we want to work with and in our case it is
 > the Tribus_run.ipynb.]{.mark}

- [Some examples of logic table -]{.mark}

 > folder (Eg: Tribus_Output)]{.mark}

**3 . NAPARI FAST MASKING**

bus_Output)]{.mark}

**3 . NAPARI FAST MASKING**

- [Once this is done, we need to create a virtual env for
 > napari]{.mark}

- [And launch the Napari_fast_masking.ipynb using the Jupyter notebook
 > and go though all the sections and then Napari should pop
 > open.]{.mark}

 > under 'impath']{.mark}

- [Upload the channels.xlsx in the channel list]{.mark}

- [Upload the 'mask' from segmentation under mask path]{.mark}

- [Upload the tribus_annotation.csv in the 'label' section and not
 > raw_annotation.]{.mark}

- [Click on 'open image' and 'show celltype masks]{.mark}

- [Now we should be able to see everything.]{.mark}

4\. How to view all heatmaps together on Napari

A[ll we need to do is change the category when we run it on the terminal
(Epithelial, lympoid, etc)]{.mark}

python /Volumes/KINGSTON/CIN_2_Experiment_1/view_heatmaps_napari.py
**Epithelial** /Volumes/KINGSTON/CIN_2_Experiment_1/tribus_output

**GATING**

\- Create a folder called 'Phenotyping' under the main directory and
create two folders 'input_data' and 'output_data'

- Under 'input_data' we need to have all the csv files after
 > Cylinyter.

- After we have our 'input_data' file, we download the

eate two folders 'input_data' and 'output_data'

- Under 'input_data' we need to have all the csv files after
 > Cylinyter.

- After we have our 'input_data' file, we download the
 > scimap_gating.ipynb from here under the 'Phenotyping' directory:
 > [[https://github.com/farkkilab/SPACE/tree/main/2_phenotyping]{.underline}](https://github.com/farkkilab/SPACE/tree/main/2_phenotyping)

- Create a csv file called 'manual_gating' and under here we need to
 > add all the manual gates we select.

The structure of this file :

Marker , Sample_1 , sampßle_2 , ...........

DNA1 , 2.4 , 9.7

Iba1 , 8.4 , 8.2

**How gating is done:**

- Open the .ipynb file and select the kernel on the right corner and
 > choose the python environment 'scimap'.

- Change the sample name under Anndata and also the paths for image
 > and output.

- Once that is done, move to the next step where we rearrange the
 > columns and in here, put the order in which you want your .csv
 > file to be.

It's usually such that it needs to start with CellID, DNA1 .... And ends
with X_centroid, Y_centroid, area, eccentricity, Majoraxis length... and
so on.

Refer Scimap_dating.ipynb

- Update the marker list under 'Define manual gate'

- After pressing play on the left, we see all the graphs for our
 > markers.

- Under this we need to update the file path for our tiff image and

list under 'Define manual gate'

- After pressing play on the left, we see all the graphs for our
 > markers.

- Under this we need to update the file path for our tiff image and
 > choose one marker after another and now we officially start the
 > gating process.

- We need to also choose a gating range from looking at the graph so
 > we can choose the right gate.

- Gating is done for one marker per sample at a time so its going to
 > be a LONG process.
